## Introduction {#sec-introduction}

The race to build artificial intelligence infrastructure is the defining capital allocation event of the 2020s.
Between 2023 and 2025, the leading AI laboratories---Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and their cloud infrastructure providers---committed over \$200 billion in 2024 alone, with combined capital expenditure from the four largest investors projected to reach \$660--690 billion in 2026 [@jassy2026confidence].
These investments are largely irreversible: a \$10 billion training cluster cannot be easily repurposed, and the competitive dynamics of AI development make delay potentially fatal.
Yet the fundamental uncertainty driving these decisions---whether and when AI capabilities will reach a transformative threshold---remains profoundly unresolved.

A frontier AI laboratory produces intelligence by combining two forms of compute. *Training compute* is used to optimize a neural network's parameters on massive datasets, yielding a model whose quality scales as a power law of compute invested [@kaplan2020scaling; @altman2025observations]. *Inference compute* runs the trained model to serve predictions and generate revenue. Both activities consume the same scarce hardware---purpose-built GPU clusters housed in multi-billion-dollar data centers---creating an intertemporal allocation problem with no close parallel in traditional capital budgeting. The infrastructure is highly irreversible: facilities take 18--24 months to build, GPU architectures depreciate on similar cycles, and algorithmic breakthroughs can devalue installed capacity overnight. Executives frame the resulting risk calculus in starkly asymmetric terms. Sundar Pichai has argued that "the risk of underinvesting is dramatically greater than the risk of overinvesting" [@pichai2024risk], while Jensen Huang contends that agentic AI and reasoning require "easily 100x more" compute than anticipated just one year prior [@huang2025scaling]. The prize these investments target---what this paper models as a regime switch to high demand---is variously called "transformative AI" or "AGI": a step change in model capability sufficient to automate a broad swath of cognitive tasks and generate sustained, qualitatively higher demand for compute [@amodei2024machines]. Executive timeline estimates for this event range from "superintelligence in a few thousand days" [@altman2024intelligence] and 2026--2027 [@amodei2024machines] to "not with current architectures" [@lecun2025autoregressive], and it is precisely this dispersion in beliefs that the revealed beliefs methodology developed below is designed to exploit.

This paper develops a unified model of irreversible capacity investment under demand uncertainty that captures the distinctive features of AI infrastructure competition.
I combine real options theory [@mcdonald1986value; @dixit1994investment] with regime switching [@guo2005investment], oligopoly competition [@grenadier2002option; @huisman2015strategic], endogenous default risk [@leland1994corporate], and diminishing returns calibrated to empirical scaling laws [@kaplan2020scaling; @hoffmann2022training].
The model delivers three main contributions.

**First**, I provide an analytical characterization of optimal investment triggers and capacity choice in a duopoly with default risk.
Firms choose both *when* to invest (the demand trigger $X^*$) and *how much* capacity to install ($K^*$), facing a Tullock contest function [@tullock1980efficient; @skaperdas1996contest] that captures the winner-take-most nature of AI markets.
I show that the interaction between strategic preemption and default risk generates a novel trade-off: firms invest earlier to preempt rivals but must limit capacity to avoid excessive leverage.
The model produces closed-form investment triggers, extending @huisman2015strategic to incorporate capacity choice with diminishing returns and endogenous default.

**Second**, I develop numerical solutions for $N$-firm sequential equilibrium with dynamic training-inference allocation.
Using backward induction [@bouis2009multistage], I solve for the entry order, capacity choices, and investment timing of $N$ heterogeneous firms.
A key feature is the training-inference frontier: firms allocate compute between model training (which improves quality according to scaling laws) and inference (which serves demand), creating an intertemporal trade-off absent from standard capacity models.
I show that the $N$-firm equilibrium converges to the duopoly solution as $N \to 2$, and that increasing competition accelerates investment but reduces individual capacity, consistent with the stylized facts of AI infrastructure buildout.

**Third**, and most novel, I develop a *revealed beliefs* methodology for inferring AI labs' private probability assessments of transformative AI from their observable investment decisions.
The key insight is that a firm's investment behavior---when it invests and how much capacity it builds---is a deterministic function of model parameters, all of which are publicly observable or can be calibrated *except* for $\lambda$, the Poisson arrival rate of a high-demand regime.
The parameter $\lambda$ encodes the firm's belief about when AI will become sufficiently capable to generate sustained demand for massive compute capacity.
By inverting the model, I back out each firm's implied $\lambda$---its *revealed belief* about AI timelines---from its observed capital expenditure.
In the spirit of @samuelson1938note, this approach extracts private information from observable choices rather than stated forecasts.

If AI labs have private information about progress---internal benchmarks, emergent capabilities, scaling projections not available to outside observers---then the revealed $\lambda$ contains information valuable to investors, policymakers, and researchers.
I calibrate the model to stylized versions of Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and CoreWeave, and show that their investment patterns imply a wide dispersion of AI timeline beliefs, with frontier labs investing as if they assign substantially higher probability to near-term transformative AI than market consensus.

I also introduce the *Dario dilemma*: the cost to a firm of investing according to a $\lambda$ that differs from its true belief. The term is motivated by Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei's candid articulation of this trade-off: "I could buy \$1 trillion of compute that starts at the end of 2027. If my revenue is not \$1 trillion dollars, if it's even \$800 billion, there's no force on Earth, there's no hedge on Earth that could stop me from going bankrupt" [@amodei2026cone].
If a firm is conservative (investing with $\lambda_{\text{invest}} < \lambda_{\text{true}}$), it invests too late and loses first-mover advantage; if aggressive ($\lambda_{\text{invest}} > \lambda_{\text{true}}$), it overbuilds and risks default.
I quantify this trade-off across the parameter space, showing that the asymmetry is substantial: aggressive overinvestment carries higher downside risk than conservative underinvestment, rationalizing the extreme capital commitments observed in practice only if firms hold genuinely optimistic beliefs about AI timelines.

{{< include _literature.qmd >}}

@fig-sample-paths illustrates the demand environment by simulating five sample paths of the demand process under regime switching.
In each realization, demand begins in the low regime $L$ and may jump to the high regime $H$ at a random Poisson time.
The dashed line marks the optimal investment trigger $X_H^*$: paths that cross this threshold in the high regime trigger investment.
The figure highlights both the uncertainty about the timing of the regime switch and the substantial variation in demand trajectories conditional on switching, which together generate the option value of waiting that is central to the analysis.

![Simulated demand paths under regime switching. Each path begins in the low regime ($L$, thin lines) and may transition to the high regime ($H$, thick lines) at a random Poisson time (marked by dots). The dashed line indicates the optimal H-regime investment trigger $X_H^*$. Parameters: $\mu_L = 0.01$, $\mu_H = 0.06$, $\sigma_L = 0.25$, $\sigma_H = 0.30$, $\lambda = 0.10$, $r = 0.12$.](figures/fig_sample_paths.pdf){#fig-sample-paths}

The paper proceeds as follows.
@sec-model presents the model environment, the single-firm benchmark, and the duopoly equilibrium with default risk.
@sec-extensions develops the $N$-firm game with training-inference allocation.
@sec-calibration describes the calibration to AI infrastructure firms.
@sec-valuation presents the revealed beliefs methodology, valuation analysis, and the Dario dilemma.
@sec-discussion considers policy implications.
@sec-conclusion concludes.
